text: The surgeon inserts instruments using a four-port robotic setup, with a monopolar hook cautery in the right hand and a robotic clip applicator repurposed for retraction in the fourth arm. The Firefly mode is used to delineate the anatomy, highlighting the stomach, duodenum, gallbladder neck, common bile duct, right hepatic artery, and cystic artery.

The surgeon begins the dissection with a small incision at the peritoneum's inferior edge, proceeding anteriorly to expose the cystic artery and cystic node. The dissection continues in a U-shaped manner to define the lower edge of the gallbladder, specifically the neck and infundibulum region. A 30-degree up view is utilized for posterior dissection, facilitated by the robotic platform.

The Ruvia sulcus is clearly visualized as the surgeon incises the peritoneum in the lower third of the gallbladder. The anterior dissection progresses with circumferential dissection and cauterization of the cystic artery. The surgeon focuses on clearing fibro-fatty tissue in Calot's triangle to create a window, ensuring a segment of about 1.5 centimeters for clip placement. The lower third of the gallbladder is delineated, revealing no aberrant ducts and a small, insignificant vessel.

The surgeon completes the circumferential dissection and isolates the cystic duct. Using the robotic clip applicator, three clips are applied: two towards the patient and one towards the specimen. The cystic duct is then divided using the monopolar hook cautery, achieving a clean cut. Due to the flabby and enlarged gallbladder, the surgeon employs an open book technique combined with a retrograde approach.

The surgeon performs an up-to-down and left-to-right dissection, entering the loose areolar plane between the gallbladder and liver. The dissection continues smoothly due to the absence of acute cholecystitis, allowing easy identification of tissue planes. The final tissue connecting the gallbladder to the liver is divided, completing the surgery.
